title: Forces & Motion
objective: Students will explore forces & motion concepts including Predicting Motion Patterns, Balanced & Unbalanced Forces.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about Predicting Motion Patterns. Get ready for an adventure!
Make observations and measurements of an object's motion to provide evidence that a pattern can be used to predict future motion
Let's practice together! Measure an object's motion (distance, speed, direction) under different conditions
If you rolls a ball down ramps of different heights, can they spot the pattern and predict how far it will roll from a new height?

Great job! Today we learned about Predicting Motion Patterns. Measure an object's motion (distance, speed, direction) under different conditions Identify a pattern in the data (e.g. steeper ramp = further roll)
Let's discover the amazing world of Balanced & Unbalanced Forces!
Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence of the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the motion of an object
Time to try this out: Define balanced forces (equal in size, opposite in direction — no change in motion) and unbalanced forces (cause motion to change)
Can you explain why a book sitting on a table doesn't move (balanced forces) but slides when you push it (unbalanced forces)?

Great job! Today we learned about Balanced & Unbalanced Forces. Define balanced forces (equal in size, opposite in direction — no change in motion) and unbalanced forces (cause motion to change) Plan a fair test investigating how different force sizes affect an object's motion
Have you ever thought about Solids, Liquids & Gases? Let's find out together!
Compare and group materials as solids, liquids, or gases based on their observable properties and behaviour
Let me show you something cool. Define solids (fixed shape, hard to compress), liquids (flow, take shape of container), gases (spread out, fill space)
Can you explain the difference between a solid like ice, a liquid like water, and a gas like steam, and sort everyday things into these three groups?

Great job! Today we learned about Solids, Liquids & Gases. Define solids (fixed shape, hard to compress), liquids (flow, take shape of container), gases (spread out, fill space) Sort at least eight everyday materials into solid, liquid, or gas categories
Have you ever thought about Evaporation & the Water Cycle? Let's find out together!
Identify the role of evaporation and condensation in the water cycle, and associate the rate of evaporation with temperature
Time to try this out: Describe evaporation as liquid turning to gas and condensation as gas turning to liquid
Can you explain why puddles disappear on a sunny day and where the water goes, and why the bathroom mirror steams up after a hot shower?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Evaporation & the Water Cycle. Describe evaporation as liquid turning to gas and condensation as gas turning to liquid Explain the water cycle: evaporation from seas/lakes → condensation into clouds → precipitation
